coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots passivated CsPbBr3 film with improved water stability and photocurrent: Preparation, characterization and application
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Jie
Song, Minxia
Chen, Lu
Shu, Yun
Jin, Dangqin
Fan, Gaochao
Xu, Qin
Hu, Xiao-Ya
coredata.dc:description: All-inorganic cesium lead perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) with excellent optical properties have obtained extensive study in the field of optoelectronics. However, the poor stability of CsPbBr3 in the atmosphere and water severely limits their practical application. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were exploited as dopants to improve the aqueous stability and photo-induced charge transfer properties of CsPbBr3. A highly fluorescent and aqueous stable film, consisting of CQDs-CsPbBr3 embedded in polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) polymer, was obtained by a simple spin coating method. Experiment results showed that the fluorescence (FL) intensity of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film retained about 85% of its initial value after being soaked in water for 50 days, indicating the good water stability of CQDs doped CsPbBr3. Besides, the photocurrent of CsPbBr3 film increased from 0.1 μA to 0.9 μA after doping CQDs under light irradiation, which proved that the improved charge transfer of CsPbBr3 was beneficial from CQDs. The application of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film in down-converted light emitting diodes has also been checked. This work highlighted one application of CQDs for improving the performances of PQDs in the field of optoelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite
Carbon quantum dots
Film
Aqueous stability
Photocurrent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CsPbBr3 and CQDs-CsPbBr3 precursor solution
Characterization of CQDs, CsPbBr3 and their composite
Optical and photoelectrochemical characters of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF
Effect of CQDs on the water-resistance and photoelectrochemical characters of CsPbBr3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF thin film preparation process.

Typical TEM image and size distribution chart (inset) of (A) CQDs, (B) CsPbBr3 PQDs and (C) CQDs-CsPbBr3 @PVDF. The insets in (B) and (C) shows HRTEM images of CsPbBr3 PQDs and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FTIR spectra of CsPbBr3 PQDs (a), CQDs (b), CQDs-CsPbBr3 (c) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (d); (B) 1H NMR spectra of CQDs (curve a), CsPbBr3 (curve b) and CQDs-CsPbBr3(curve c); XPS spectra of CsPbBr3 PQDs and CQDs@ CsPbBr3 for survey (C), Cs 3d (D), Pb 4f (E), Br 3d (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of (A) CsPbBr3, (B) CsPbBr3 @PVDF, (C) CQDs-CsPbBr3@ PVDF films (the insets were the magnifications). (D) XRD patterns of CsPbBr3@ PVDF film (a) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FL spectra of CsPbBr3 doped with different amounts of CQDs; (B) The absorption (dashed lines) and normalized FL (solid lines) spectra of CsPbBr3 solution (a), CQDs-CsPbBr3 solution (b) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF solution (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Water contact angle measurements of (A) CsPbBr3@PVDF and (B) CQDs-CsPbBr3 @PVDF. (C) Normalized FL intensity of CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve a) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve b) films soaked in water for a period of time. The insets show the digital photographs of CsPbBr3@PVDF and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film immersed in water under UV irradiation after 50 days. (D) Photocurrent response curves of CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve a) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve b) composite films in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A) FL emission spectra and (B) the CIE color coordinates of down-converted LEDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots passivated CsPbBr3 film with improved water stability and photocurrent: Preparation, characterization and application

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, 225002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All-inorganic cesium lead perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) with excellent optical properties have obtained extensive study in the field of optoelectronics. However, the poor stability of CsPbBr3 in the atmosphere and water severely limits their practical application. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were exploited as dopants to improve the aqueous stability and photo-induced charge transfer properties of CsPbBr3. A highly fluorescent and aqueous stable film, consisting of CQDs-CsPbBr3 embedded in polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) polymer, was obtained by a simple spin coating method. Experiment results showed that the fluorescence (FL) intensity of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film retained about 85% of its initial value after being soaked in water for 50 days, indicating the good water stability of CQDs doped CsPbBr3. Besides, the photocurrent of CsPbBr3 film increased from 0.1 μA to 0.9 μA after doping CQDs under light irradiation, which proved that the improved charge transfer of CsPbBr3 was beneficial from CQDs. The application of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film in down-converted light emitting diodes has also been checked. This work highlighted one application of CQDs for improving the performances of PQDs in the field of optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, all-inorganic perovskite quantum dots (ABX3, X = Cl, Br, I), as a rising star material with excellent optical properties, have shown great application prospects in photovoltaic fields such as light-emitting diodes, solar cells, photoelectric detection, lasers, and so on [1–3]. Although PQDs have excellent photophysical properties such as adjustable band gaps, high molar extinction coefficients, and excellent charge-transfer performance, they are unstable and usually begin to degrade from the defects on the surface and grain boundaries [4,5]. To improve the stability of PQDs, the following routes are mainly employed: (i) protective coating with inorganic nanomaterials such as SiO2 [6], Al2O3 [7], TiO2 [8] et al., (ii) surface ligand passivation [9,10], (iii) encapsulating in different polymers such as PMMA [11] and PVDF [12].

Polymer protective methods have shown advantages because of the simple preparation process. However, most polymers are electrical insulators, which hinder the transport of electrons and thus weaken the performances of polymer/PQDs. Carbon-based materials (such as CNT, graphene, carbon fiber, etc.) have been proved to improve the charge transfer in many electrical and electronic applications [13,14]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with strong design ability and adjustable electronic structure have been applied as hole transport materials in solar devices [15]. Pb2+ can accept lone pairs of electrons from Lewis bases containing S, N and O [16], so CQDs with abundant -OH, -C=O and -COOH groups can act as Lewis bases to effectively combine with Pb2+ [17]. Besides, CQDs has been proved to improve the electron mobility of semiconductors such as SnO2 [18]. Therefore, CQDs may not only improve the stability of PQDs but also act as electron carriers to facilitate charge separation of PQDs at perovskite/polymers interfaces when they are used as dopants for PQDs [19].

Herein, we reported a simple method to prepare CsPbBr3 films with improved stability and electron transfer properties by using CQDs as dopants at room temperature. Fig. 1 illustrated the CsPbBr3 PQDs film preparation process and the function of CQDs. PQDs were prepared via a simple crystallization method [20] at room temperature with some adjustments. CQDs were added into CsBr and PbBr2 precursors simultaneously. The -OH, -C=O and -COOH groups on CQDs would interact with Pb2+ to stabilize them. Then, CQDs-CsPbBr3 embedded in PVDF matrix was prepared by a simple spin-coating process. The structure and FL characters of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film were studied. Experiments results proved that the hybrid combinations have superior characteristics regarding excellent water stability, separation of charge carriers, and excellent electrical conductivity. The quality of the developed CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite based light-emitting diodes (LEDs) has also been studied.

The indium tin oxide coated glass (ITO, 8 Ω/square) used in this study were purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd (Zhuhai, China). Lead bromide (PbBr2, 99%), cesium bromide (CsBr, 99.5%), oleic acid (OA, 90%), PVDF were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Oleylamine (OAm, 90%) was purchased from Macklin Inc (Shanghai, China). Toluene, citric acid, urea, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (analytical purity) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification. Dialysis membranes Spectra/Por® 7 (molecular weight cutoff 1000) (Carlroth, Germany) were used as received.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained on a Zeiss Supra 55 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were recorded on a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (TENSOR27, Germany). The contact angle data was obtained from an OCA 20 optical contact angle meter (Dataphysics, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The FL spectra were measured on a FS5 Edinburgh Spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, England). The photoelectrochemical experiments were recorded on a CIMPS-2 workstation (Zennium, Zahner-ElektrikGmbH & CoKG, Germany) by using a 500 W WLC02 (a type of white light source) ((4300 k) # 1522, Zahner-Elektrik) as the visible irradiation source. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) analyses were recorded on Avance III HD 400 MHz Spectrometer (Bruker) in dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (Aldrich).

The CQDs using citric acid and urea as precursors were synthesized via a solvothermal route in DMF [21]. In a typical synthesis process, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were completely dissolved in DMF (10 mL). The temperature of the mixture was kept at 160 °C for 6 h under solvothermal condition. After naturally cooled down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed against continuously running deionized water through dialysis membranes (molecular weight cutoff 1000) for 24 h for further purification, and then the dialysate was evaporated to dryness to obtain the purified CQDs powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CsPbBr3 and CQDs-CsPbBr3 precursor solution
ce:section.ce:para: The CsPbBr3 solution was prepared via a previously reported method [20] with a little modification. Firstly, PbBr2 (0.4 mmol) and CsBr (0.4 mmol) were dissolved in a DMF and DMSO (10 ml, volume ratio of 4: 1) mixture at room temperature. Then, OA (1 mL) and OAm (0.5 mL) were injected. The temperature of this mixture was increased to 80 °C and was held constant for 2 h.
The CQDs-CsPbBr3 precursor preparation process was similar to that of CsPbBr3 precursor except for the addition of CQDs into the precursor solution before the addition of OA and OAm.

Polymer PVDF (200 mg) was added into the CQDs-CsPbBr3 precursor solution with physical stirring and blending at 80 °C, and then the prepared mixture was spin-coated on a quartz or an ITO glass at a speed of 750 r/min for 60s. Before using, the quartz and ITO glasses were sequentially cleaned with acetone, absolute ethanol, deionized water in an ultrasonic bath each for 15 min, and then dried. Thereafter, the films were left to dry for 30 min at ambient temperature before annealing at 70 °C for 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, CsPbBr3 and their composite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A and B were the TEM images of CQDs and CsPbBr3 PQDs. Insets were their size distribution diagrams. Both of them showed uniform shapes and narrow size distributions. The average size of CQDs and CsPbBr3 PQDs were about 2 nm and 10 nm, respectively. Fig. 2 C provided a typical TEM image of the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite. It could be observed that the formed CQDs- CsPbBr3 nanocomposites were embedded in the PVDF matrix with the size of 8 nm. Compared with CsPbBr3 PQDs, the diameter of the formed composite was a little smaller. It may be due to the reason that CQDs can effectively passivate the surface defects of CsPbBr3 and control their crystal growth [22]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis of CsPbBr3 and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (inset in Fig. 2B and C) clearly showed that the interface spaces of pure CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3 in CQDs- CsPbBr3@PVDF were 0.29 nm and 0.41 nm, respectively. They were corresponded to the (200) (0.291 nm) [23] and (110) (0.413 nm) [24] crystal planes of CsPbBr3 measured by XRD.

Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were used to study the interaction between CQDs and CsPbBr3 and explored the formation of CQDs-CsPbBr3. The distinct characteristic peaks of CsPbBr3 PQDs (Fig. 3 A, curve a) around 1456, 1700, and 2900 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C-O, C=O, and C-H of OA and OAm [25]. For CQDs (Fig. 3A, curve b), the band located around 3400 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of the -OH group, and the peak at 2850 cm−1 was ascribed to N-H bonds. The stretching vibration bands of C=O groups appeared at 1714 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1. The C-N bending stretching vibration peak was also observed at 1182 cm−1 [26]. Besides, the chemical structures of CQDs-CsPbBr3 (Fig. 3A, curve c) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (Fig. 3A, curve d) were examined by FTIR. Compared with curve b, the characteristic peak of the C=O stretching vibrations bond in curve c shifted from 1714 cm−1–1732 cm−1, which indicated a strong interaction between CQDs and CsPbBr3. The lone pair electrons on the carboxylic group of CQDs delocalized to the empty orbit of Pb2+ in CsPbBr3 [27], which was helpful for the formation of stable perovskite films. For CQDs- CsPbBr3@PVDF, the peak at 1406 cm−1 was the deformation vibration absorption peak of CH2 connected to CF2 in PVDF and 1193 cm−1 was the stretching vibration absorption peak of CF2 [28], indicating the presence of PVDF in the composite.

In order to better understand the role of CQDs in perovskite materials, we have performed a series of NMR experiments in solution. The 1H NMR spectrum of CQDs-CsPbBr3 in the deuterated solvent DMSO was recorded and compared with those of CQDs and CsPbBr3 (Fig. 3 B). In Fig. 3 B, curve a, b and c were the 1H NMR spectra of CQDs, CsPbBr3 and CQDs-CsPbBr3, respectively. The signal appeared at δ = ∼2.69 ppm in curve a was from citric acid on the surface of the obtained CQDs because of the incomplete condensation and carbonization process during CQDs preparation [29]. For CsPbBr3 (curve b), the resonances at δ = ∼3.20 were related to the –NH2 of OAm [30]. Primary amines were generally used to improve the stability of lead halide perovskite nanocrystals. However, the adsorption of OAm on CQDs surface was affected by many factors. The adsorbed protons or other unwashed Lewis acid species would cause the desorption of OAm from CsPbBr3 surface and induce the colloidal instability [31]. Compared to the 1H NMR spectrum of CQDs, the typical resonances at δ = ∼2.69 ppm disappeared at the 1H NMR spectrum of CQDs-CsPbBr3. Furthermore, the resonances at δ = ∼3.20 which were related to -NH2 decreased, which meant OAm was replaced by CQDs through the interaction between Pb2+ and hydroxyl and carbonyl ligand. The loss of structural integrity of CsPbBr3 because of the desorption of OAm from CsPbBr3 surface would be alleviated when CQDs were doped, and the stability of CsPbBr3 was improved.

We further carried out XPS analysis to study the interaction between CQDs and CsPbBr3 PQDs. Fig. 3C showed the full XPS spectra of CQDs-CsPbBr3 and CsPbBr3 powder samples, from which the peaks of Cs 3d, Pb 4f, and Br 3d displayed clearly. This meant that the complex contained the elements expected from CsPbBr3. The ratio of Cs: Pb: Br in CQDs-CsPbBr3 was determined to be 1:1:3, indicating CQDs doping didn’t change the stoichiometry of CsPbBr3 PQDs. The peaks at 724.5 and 738.3 eV were attributed to the binding energies of Cs 3d5/2 and Cs 3d3/2 (Fig. 3D). The Pb 4f spectrum of the original CsPbBr3 was recorded(Fig. 3E), where the two contributors 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 were located at 138.10 and 142.96 eV, respectively [32]. Two Br 3d signals corresponding to the peaks of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 could also be clearly seen at 68.3 eV and 69.3 eV in the Br 3d spectrum (Fig. 3F) [22]. Notably, comparing to CsPbBr3, the peak positions of Cs 3d and Pb 4f in CQDs-CsPbBr3 shifted slightly to lower binding energies. This result further indicated that CQDs interacted with CsPbBr3 through chemical bonds. Both Cs and Pb obtained the lone pair of electrons provided by C=O or C-N group on the surface of CQDs. which was conducive to the formation of a more stable perovskite crystal structure [17].

It is well known that PVDF can swell and expand in DMF. During the expansion process, CQDs-CsPbBr3 precursor was introduced into the PVDF matrix. When DMF was driven out of the polymer matrix, PVDF would shrink and form a protective layer around CQDs-CsPbBr3, protecting them from water, oxygen or heat [33]. The morphologies of CsPbBr3 PQDs, CsPbBr3@PVDF and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF films were characterized by SEM. Fig. 4 A showed the SEM image of pure CsPbBr3 PQDs film, where rough surface and large gaps between the grains could be observed. The CsPbBr3@PVDF composites film showed reduced grain size, surface roughness and gaps (Fig. 4B). In the presence of CQDs and PVDF, the CsPbBr3 film was composed of denser perovskite grains with reduced pinholes and defects(Fig. 4C). The dangling bonds on CQDs (hydrophilic groups like -OH, -C=O, and -COOH) contributed to the excellent linkages of CQDs with CsPbBr3 [27], so the obtained CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film demonstrated much more compact crystal grains and the gaps between the grains became smaller than that of the pristine CsPbBr3 film. Besides, compared with the pristine CsPbBr3 film., the grain size of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF was smaller, which was consistent with the TEM data. This size reduction indicated the higher nucleation density of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film.

The crystal structures of CsPbBr3@PVDF and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite films were characterized by XRD (Fig. 4 D). Both of the two films showed strong scattered signals. The peak at 21° was a characteristic peak of PVDF [34]. The main diffraction peaks of the two films (Fig. 4D, curve a and b) at 15.2°, 21.5°, 30.6°, 34.4°, 37.6° and 43.6° were corresponded to (100), (110), (200), (210), (121) and (202) crystal faces of the cubic phase of CsPbBr3, respectively, which were similar to the previously reported works [35]. No other peaks were observed. This meant that only cubic structure CsPbBr3 was presented in the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film. The lower content and smaller size of CQDs would not change the structure of CsPbBr3 PQDs. Similar results have also been reported by others [36]. Besides, after doping with CQDs, the peak intensity of CsPbBr3 decreased, which was due to the extremely suppressed crystal growth that has been proven by SEM and TEM characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelectrochemical characters of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF
ce:section.ce:para: FL spectra were used to study the effect of the doped CQDs amount on the quality of CsPbBr3 PQDs. Fig. 5 A showed the FL emission spectra of the CQDs-CsPbBr3 solution with various concentrations of CQDs. Each of them showed a highly symmetric band centered at around 505 nm, indicating the outstanding green emission performance of the obtained CsPbBr3. The FL emission peak intensity of CsPbBr3 increased with the increase of CQDs amount. It reached a maximum when the concentration of CQDs was 0.15 mg/ml. The increase of FL intensity meant that few non-radiative recombination happened. We attributed this small non-radiative recombination loss to the passivation defect effect of CQDs. Further increase of CQDs amount in the solution would cause a decrease of the FL intensity. This is probably because excessive CQDs in the composite would be acted as non-radiative recombination centers and thus reduce the FL intensity [37].

Fig. 5B displayed the absorption (dashed lines) and normalized FL spectra of CsPbBr3, CQDs-CsPbBr3 and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF solution, respectively. An absorption peak at 520 nm was observed for CsPbBr3, corresponding to a bandgap of 2.3 eV (dashed line, curve a). When CQDs were doped, the absorption peak was shifted to 505 nm (dashed line, curve b and c) and the band gap increased to 2.5 eV. In addition, the incorporation of CQDs resulted in the blue shift of the emission peak of CsPbBr3 (solid line, curve b and c). This meant that CQDs can inhibit the growth of CsPbBr3, matching the above description of TEM. Furthermore, Fig. 5B showed that the full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission band of CsPbBr3 was about 25 nm. It increased to 28 nm when CQDs were introduced. Compared with CQDs-CsPbBr3, the FWHM of CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF increased to 35 nm. The spectral diffusion has been considered to exert effects on the emission linewidth broadening [38], which ultimately determined the color purity of CsPbBr3. Spectral diffusion is the environmentally induced fluctuations in the emission energy of individual nanoparticles. It would help us to understand the local environments around fluorescence materials and study energy transfer between their localized states [39]. The broadening of FWHM told us CsPbBr3 became more sensitive to local electric field fluctuations in the presence of CQDs and PVDF. The color purity of CsPbBr3 changed a little but stability was improved dramatically in the presence of CQDs and PVDF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on the water-resistance and photoelectrochemical characters of CsPbBr3
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CsPbBr3 PQDs in an aqueous solution is a major issue that should be overcome for the application of perovskite. In this work, we investigated the water resistance and storage stability of different composite materials to investigate the effect of CQDs for improving the aqueous stability of CsPbBr3. When CsPbBr3 PQDs and polymer PVDF were directly blended, the water contact angle of the CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film was 114° (Fig. 6 A), indicating good hydrophobicity. The polymer PVDF encapsulation increased the hydrophobicity of the perovskite. After doping with CQDs, the contact angle increased to 124° (Fig. 6B), showing that CQDs could further improve the hydrophobicity of CsPbBr3.

The prepared CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film was immersed in water for a period of time to measure its water stability. A CsPbBr3@PVDF thin film was tested at the same time for comparison. Fig. 6C showed the slightly decrease of the FL intensities of both CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve a) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (curve b) in the first 2–3 days, which was mainly related to the luminescence quenching of loosely embedded CsPbBr3 in PVDF. Then, the FL intensities became stable. Even after being soaked in aqueous solution for 50 days, both films still maintained about 80% of their initial emission intensity. The inset of Fig. 6C showed the brightness of CsPbBr3@PVDF and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF films after being soaking in water for 50 days. The CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film was brighter than CsPbBr3@PVDF film. Fig. 6C sowed that the FL efficiency of the film doped with CQDs (85%) was about 7% higher than that of the perovskite composite film in the absence of CQDs (78%) after 50 days storage in water. The doping with CQDs increased the water stability of the CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film.

The effect of CQDs on the performances of CsPbBr3 was checked by testing the photocurrents responses of CsPbBr3@PVDF film (Fig. 6D curve a) and CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film (Fig. 6D curve b) in Na2SO4 solution under visible light irradiation. Fig. 6D showed the stable photocurrents of both films. Yet, the photocurrents on CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF (0.9 μA) were higher than those on CsPbBr3@PVDF (0.1 μA). The stable but low photocurrent meant that PVDF could well isolate the contact between the CsPbBr3 and the aqueous solution, but limited the transfer of electrons. After doping with CQDs, the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF film not only showed improved stability in the aqueous solution but also had a larger photocurrent. The uniform and dense morphology, smaller grain size, and higher film quality of CQDs-CsPbBr3 @PVDF film made the electrical performance and charge transport capability of the film better [40]. In addition, the increase of the band gap due to the doped CQDs made the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes difficult, which was beneficial to the occurrence of the photoelectric effect [41]. Therefore, when CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF was used as a photoelectric element, CQDs accelerated the electron extraction and charge transfer of the composite film, and promoted the separation of photo-generated charges when being illuminated [42].

PQDs have been proved to be an ideal material for LEDs. The down-converted LED was built up by coating the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite on an ultraviolet chip. Fig. 7 A showed the FL spectra of the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film. Inset of Fig. 7A showed that the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film covered LED emitted bright green light. According to the FL spectrum of the film, the color coordinates (0.0703, 0.5139) were plotted in the CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. 7B, which was consistent with the green light observed by the naked eye and met the requirements of CIE coordinates in display applications.

In this study, a simple and low-cost method was developed to prepare perovskite composite films with improve aqueous stability and photoelectrochemical characters. The doping of CQDs passivated the lattice defects on the surface of CsPbBr3 and made it more stable. After being soaked in water for more than 50 days, the FL efficiency of the prepared CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film could keep 85% of its initial value. Besides, CQDs improved the photo-generated electron and hole separation ability of CsPbBr3 and provided charge collection channels to promote electron extraction, which made the CQDs-CsPbBr3@PVDF composite film can be stably subjected to photoelectric testing in aqueous solution. The prepared CQDs-CsPbBr3@ PVDF composite film has also been proved to be suitable for the field of LEDs. We believe that improving the stability and charge separation of CsPbBr3 with the help of CQDs can broaden its application in optoelectronic devices.

Jie Guan: Funding acquisition, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, acquisition of data, analysis and/or interpretation of data, Drafting the manuscript, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Minxia Song: Funding acquisition, Data curation, acquisition of data, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Lu Chen: Funding acquisition, Data curation, acquisition of data, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Yun Shu: Data curation, Formal analysis, analysis and/or interpretation of data, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Dangqin Jin: Funding acquisition, Data curation, acquisition of data, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Gaochao Fan: Data curation, Formal analysis, analysis and/or interpretation of data, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Qin Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Conception and design of study, analysis and/or interpretation of data, Drafting the manuscript, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Xiao-Ya Hu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Conception and design of study, analysis and/or interpretation of data, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank NSFC (21675140, 22076161 and 21705141), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20181219), the 14th Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province (SWYY-085), Young academic leaders of Jiangsu Province (2018), Talent Support Program of Yangzhou University, Higher Education Outstanding Scientific and Technological Innovation Team of Jiangsu Province (2017-6), the Open Project Program of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Zoonosis (No. R2013), the Yangzhou City and Yangzhou University cooperation fund (YZ2020184), the project funded by the PAPD and TAPP. We also thank the Test Center of Yangzhou University for the SEM and TEM characterizations and Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis for their support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-inorganic perovskite quantum dot/TiO2 inverse opal electrode platform: stable and efficient photoelectrochemical sensing of dopamine under visible irradiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, D. Li, G. Pan, D. Zhou, W. Xu, J. Zhu, , et al., All-inorganic perovskite quantum dot/TiO2 inverse opal electrode platform: stable and efficient photoelectrochemical sensing of dopamine under visible irradiation, Nanoscale (2018); 10(22): 10505-10513.
